commentary.hc2:
Headline CPI ran at +3.73% YoY in June 2026 while core (ex food and energy) came in at +2.81% YoY, with both measures essentially flat on a monthly basis (-0.42% and -0.02%, respectively).

The 93-basis-point gap between headline and core reflects energy's outsized annual contribution: the energy index was up +15.95% YoY even as it fell sharply month-over-month (-5.71%), with gasoline alone up +26.72% YoY and electricity +24.33% YoY, suggesting the year-over-year energy drag on core is not yet fading but the monthly impulse has reversed.

Shelter (+3.48% YoY, +0.12% MoM) remained the largest persistent core component, while vehicle-adjacent categories were mixed—new vehicles nearly flat (+0.50% YoY), used vehicles in mild deflation (-1.77% YoY), and motor vehicle insurance in outright annual deflation (-3.47% YoY) after prior outsized gains; motor vehicle maintenance & repair, however, continued to run
commentary.mfg2:
Electricity costs rose sharply at 24.33% year-over-year, representing the largest increase among the four components by a considerable margin. Natural gas posted a more moderate gain of 4.53%, while household furnishings increased 3.23% and food at home rose 2.93%, the three non-electricity components clustering within roughly one percentage point of each other.

• Tire industry: The outsized electricity inflation of 24.33% directly pressures energy-intensive tire manufacturing operations—including mixing, curing, and vulcanization processes—where electricity is a primary input cost, potentially compressing margins if the increase cannot be passed through to customers.
commentary.transp2:
Gasoline and airline fares posted sharp year-over-year increases of 26.72% and 26.97%, respectively, while motor vehicle insurance fell 3.47% and fuel oil declined modestly by 0.95%. The divergence places the heaviest upward pressure on road and air passenger transport, whereas distillate-dependent freight sectors face a comparatively stable fuel cost environment.

• Tire industry: The pronounced gasoline price increase raises vehicle operating costs for fleets and consumers, which can suppress discretionary driving miles and moderate demand for replacement tires in the passenger segment.
commentary.fed2:
Headline inflation runs 186 basis points above the 2% target on a year-over-year basis, while core CPI at 2.81% YoY indicates that underlying price pressures remain elevated even after stripping out food and energy. The negative monthly print of −0.42% introduces a disinflationary data point that will complicate the signal, as it may reflect transitory factors rather than a sustained downward trend, and the Fed will weigh it against the still-elevated annual readings. With core materially above target and headline deceleration present but not yet confirmed as durable, the data is consistent with a Fed that holds rates at a restrictive level rather than pivoting, though the MoM softness marginally reduces urgency for further tightening.

• Tire industry: Persistently elevated core inflation keeps input costs—synthetic rubber, carbon black, and energy-intensive manufacturing—under upward pressure, while the negative MoM headline print may offer only limited and uncertain near-term relief on raw
commentary.lens2:
Headline inflation ran at 3.73% year-over-year in June 2026, pulled above the core reading of 2.81% primarily by energy. Gasoline surged 26.72% year-over-year, electricity 24.33%, and airline fares 26.97%, making transportation-related energy costs the dominant source of price pressure in the period. On a monthly basis, energy categories retreated sharply—gasoline fell 9.69% and electricity 6.64% month-over-month—suggesting some near-term easing in those components, though the annual base remains highly elevated.

Food added modest but persistent upward pressure, with Food at Home up 2.93% year-over-year and a slight 0.19% monthly gain, indicating grocery-aisle inflation has not reversed. Shelter continued its slow grind at 3.48% year-over-year. Motor vehicle maintenance and repair accelerated to 7.28% year-over-year with a 1.09% monthly increase, standing out as a hardening cost for vehicle owners. By contrast, used cars and trucks declined 1.77% year-over-year, new vehicles were nearly flat at +0.50%, and motor vehicle insurance fell 3.47% year-over-year, providing some offset within the vehicle-ownership basket.

Core inflation at 2.81% remains above the conventional 2% benchmark but is running well below headline, confirming that volatile energy prices—not broad demand-side pressure—are driving the gap.

---

commentary.tire2:
**Auto & Tire CPI Channel — June 2026**

• New vehicle prices rose 0.50% year-over-year, a modest positive reading that signals continued but subdued pricing power at the OEM and dealer level, consistent with a market that has largely normalized from the supply-shock era of 2021–2023.

• Used vehicle prices contracted 1.77% year-over-year, extending the deflationary trend in the secondary market, while motor vehicle insurance fell 3.47% year-over-year—a notable reversal after the sustained double-digit premium increases recorded through 2024 and into 2025.

• Motor vehicle maintenance & repair inflation ran at 7.28% year-over-year, the strongest reading in the channel by a wide margin, indicating that labor costs, parts prices, and service demand in the aftermarket remain significantly elevated relative to the broader vehicle-price complex.

• **Tire industry:** The 7.28% surge in motor vehicle maintenance & repair CPI—the dominant signal in this channel—points to sustained aftermarket pricing strength, which supports volume and margin in the replacement tire segment even as softer new- and used-vehicle price trends limit incremental OE fitment demand.
commentary.ppi2:
Final demand producer prices rose 6.42% year-over-year and 1.06% month-over-month in May 2026, the fastest annual pace in recent memory and above the prior core readings.

**Goods vs. Services split.** Final demand goods (+10.25% YoY, +2.78% MoM) are running more than twice as hot as final demand services (+4.89% YoY, +0.33% MoM). Within goods, energy is the dominant driver at +35.84% YoY and +10.70% MoM; even stripping out foods and energy, goods prices are up 5.12% YoY. Services inflation is broadening but more moderate: transportation & warehousing leads at +14.22% YoY, while trade services and the residual services category both sit near 4.2% YoY; trade services actually retreated 1.08% on the month, the one notable deceleration in the report.

**Pipeline (intermediate demand).** Upstream price pressure is running well ahead of final demand. Processed goods for intermediate demand are up 13.13% YoY (+3.53% MoM) and unprocessed goods for intermediate demand are up 22.41% YoY (+4.92% MoM), signaling that cost
commentary.ppilens2:
Synthetic rubber producer prices rose 17.78% year-over-year in May 2026, running well ahead of downstream tire and rubber product categories. Passenger car tire prices increased 3.57% YoY and the broader tires & tubes index moved up 2.95% YoY, both materially below the synthetic rubber input rate. Rubber & plastic products as a whole posted 4.18% YoY inflation, slightly above the finished tire indexes but far below the synthetic rubber figure.

• Synthetic rubber PPI stands at +17.78% YoY (NSA, May 2026), the sharpest rate of increase in the input cost stack by a wide margin.
• Finished tire categories—passenger car tires at +3.57% YoY and tires & tubes at +2.95% YoY (NSA, May 2026)—are rising at roughly one-fifth the pace of their primary feedstock.
• Rubber & plastic products PPI at +4.18% YoY (NSA, May 2026) sits between the synthetic rubber input and the finished tire outputs, consistent with partial pass-through across the broader rubber and plastics manufacturing chain.

• Tire industry: The 14–15 percentage-point gap between synthetic rubber input cost inflation (+17.78%) and finished passenger car tire price inflation (+3.57%) indicates that tire producers are currently absorbing a substantial portion of feedstock cost increases rather than passing them through to output prices, compressing per-unit margins on rubber-intensive product lines.
